item.title: The World Cup's American Climax: Your Guide to the Final Two Weeks
item.body:
After nearly a month and more than a hundred matches, the biggest World Cup ever staged has reached the point where everything is sudden death. The expanded 48-team tournament — spread across the United States, Mexico, and Canada — is now down to its knockout endgame, and the road to the trophy runs entirely through American stadiums from here.

## The schedule from here

- **Round of 16** — early July, wrapping up across the host map as the field narrows to eight.
- **Quarterfinals — July 9–11** in Boston (Foxborough), Los Angeles (Inglewood), Miami Gardens, and Kansas City.
- **Semifinals — July 14 in Arlington, Texas and July 15 in Atlanta**, two of the tournament's biggest venues.
- **Third-place match — July 18** in Miami Gardens.
- **The Final — Sunday, July 19, MetLife Stadium** in East Rutherford, New Jersey, in front of more than 80,000 fans and a global TV audience projected in the billions.

## What's different about this World Cup

The jump from 32 to 48 teams added an extra knockout round and made this the longest World Cup in history — 104 matches in all. Critics worried the group stage would drag; instead, the expanded field produced genuine upsets and gave first-time qualifiers their moment. But the trade-off arrives now: from the round of 16 onward, favorites have almost no margin for error, and squad depth — after five or six matches in summer heat — matters more than ever.

Heat itself has been a defining storyline. With afternoon kickoffs scheduled to serve European TV audiences, cooling breaks and hydration protocols have become as much a part of match tactics as set pieces. Teams that rotated their squads early are being rewarded late.

## How to watch it like a local

You don't need a ticket to feel it. Every host city is running official fan festivals with giant screens, and sports bars in immigrant neighborhoods — Salvadoran, Nigerian, Korean, Moroccan, you name it — have turned into the best free entertainment in America this month. Watching a knockout match in a packed room full of fans with skin in the game is the closest thing to being in the stadium.

## Why this one matters

The last time the U.S. hosted a World Cup, in 1994, it left behind Major League Soccer and a generation of American fans. This one — bigger, louder, and climaxing in the New York area — is a once-in-a-generation event on American soil. Whoever lifts the trophy on July 19, the next two weeks are the kind of sports theater you'll be telling people you watched live.
